title: Causality: confounders and colliders
intro: "Correlation ≠ causation" is the slogan everyone repeats. But what do you DO with it? When does controlling for a variable save the conclusion, and when does it ruin it? Let's work through the three basic causal structures on three nodes X, Y, Z. This turns the slogan into a working skill.

beats.text: The first structure — the confounder: Z affects both X and Y. Then a relationship appears between X and Y even though X does not affect Y. Heat (Z) raises both ice cream sales (X) and drownings (Y). Without controlling for Z, a spurious relationship is visible.
beats.predict: Press "control for Z" with the "confounder" structure. What happens to the spurious X–Y relationship?
beats.reveal: It disappears — and rightly so. Comparing days with the same heat (fixing Z), we see that ice cream and drownings are unrelated. Controlling for a confounder is the main weapon against spurious correlations (this is what multiple regression does).

beats.text: The second structure — the collider: X and Y both affect Z (a shared CONSEQUENCE, not cause). X and Y themselves are independent.
beats.predict: Switch to "collider" and press "control for Z". What happens to the X–Y relationship?
beats.reveal: A SPURIOUS relationship appears that wasn't there without the control! Controlling for a collider creates distortion. The classic: among university admits (Z), talent (X) and connections (Y) are negatively related even though they are independent in the population — because we are looking only at those admitted.

beats.text: The third structure — the mediator (a chain): X → Z → Y. Here Z is the link through which X affects Y.
beats.predict: On the "mediator" structure, switch on the control for Z. What happens to the real effect of X on Y?
beats.reveal: It disappears — we blocked the very path the effect traveled. By controlling for a mediator, it is easy to accidentally "hide" real influence. Usually the mediator is NOT controlled for if you want to see the full effect of X.

beats.text: The main takeaway: the same move — controlling for Z — gives opposite results in different structures. A confounder MUST be controlled; a collider MUST NOT (it creates a lie); a mediator — depends on the question. So "let's add all the variables to the model" is a dangerous idea: you can introduce bias with your own hands.

So how is causality established? The gold standard is the randomized experiment (A/B): random assignment severs all arrows coming into X, killing every confounder at once. When an experiment is impossible, you lean on the causal diagram: control confounders, leave colliders alone — and honestly admit the residual uncertainty.

definitions.text: a common cause of X and Y. Creates a spurious relationship; it must be controlled for.
a common consequence of X and Y. Controlling for a collider creates a spurious relationship — it must not be controlled for.
the intermediate link X → Z → Y. Controlling for a mediator blocks the real effect.
a graph of "cause → effect" arrows; it tells you what to control for and what not to.

practice: The practical skill: before "adding a control", draw the arrows. Is the variable a common cause? Control for it. A common consequence (a collider)? Absolutely not. A link in the effect? Depends on the question. Mindlessly controlling for "everything" is as dangerous as controlling for nothing.

The classic collider in product work is analyzing "active users only" or "survivors only": selecting on a consequence breeds paradoxical relationships (this IS the survivorship bias from the traps module).
realLife: In medicine, causal diagrams decide which factors go into a treatment-effect model: age and severity are confounders (control), while a treatment complication is a collider (do not control).
The famous "paradoxes" (why talent and looks seem at odds among movie stars) are collider effects born of selection, not real relationships.
assumptions: Any causal conclusion from observational data rests on an ASSUMED diagram of arrows, which cannot be verified from the data alone. Got the diagram wrong — got wrong what to control for.
Controlling removes only the ACCOUNTED-FOR confounders. There may always remain an unknown one — which is why an experiment beats any observational adjustment.
